When working with Docker containers in development:
- Use multi-stage builds to keep production images small
- Never run containers as root in production
- Use .dockerignore to exclude unnecessary files from build context
- Pin base image versions for reproducibility
- Use health checks in Docker Compose
- Mount source code as volumes for hot reloading in development
- Use Docker networks for service isolation
- Keep images under 500MB when possible
Docker dev: multi-stage builds, non-root in prod, .dockerignore, pin base versions, health checks in compose, volume mount for hot reload, Docker networks for isolation, images <500MB.

You are tasked with implementing a comprehensive backup and disaster recovery plan for our production infrastructure. This includes:
- Automated daily backups of all databases with point-in-time recovery capability
- Cross-region replication for critical data stores
- Regular backup verification by performing test restores
- Documented runbooks for common failure scenarios
- Recovery Time Objective (RTO) of 4 hours and Recovery Point Objective (RPO) of 1 hour
- Annual disaster recovery drills
- Encrypted backups with separate key management
DR plan: daily DB backups with PITR, cross-region replication, test restores regularly, failure runbooks, RTO 4h / RPO 1h, annual DR drills, encrypted backups with separate key mgmt.

When implementing internationalization (i18n) in the application:
- Extract all user-facing strings into translation files
- Use ICU message format for handling plurals, gender, and number formatting
- Support RTL languages with proper layout mirroring
- Implement locale-aware date, time, and currency formatting
- Allow dynamic language switching without page reload
- Use language detection from browser settings with user override
- Implement fallback chains (specific locale -> language -> default)
- Keep translation keys organized by feature or page

i18n: extract strings to translation files, ICU format (plurals/gender/numbers), RTL support, locale-aware date/time/currency, dynamic language switch (no reload), detect from browser + user override, fallback chain (locale->lang->default), organize keys by feature.

Please implement a comprehensive health check endpoint for our microservice that verifies:
- Database connectivity and query response time
- Redis cache availability and latency
- External API dependencies are reachable
- Disk space is above minimum threshold
- Memory usage is within acceptable bounds
- Message queue connection is healthy
The endpoint should return a structured JSON response with individual component statuses, overall health status, and response time. It should be accessible without authentication for load balancer integration.

Health check endpoint: verify DB (connectivity+latency), Redis, external APIs, disk space, memory, message queue. Return JSON with per-component status + overall health + response time. No auth (for LB).

Build a recommendation engine for our e-commerce platform. The system should:
- Use collaborative filtering based on user purchase history and browsing behavior
- Implement content-based filtering using product attributes
- Combine both approaches in a hybrid model
- Support real-time recommendations that update as users browse
- Handle the cold start problem for new users with popularity-based fallbacks
- A/B test different recommendation algorithms
- Track recommendation click-through rates and conversion rates
- Update the model daily with new data

Recommendation engine: collaborative filtering (purchases+browsing) + content-based (product attrs) in hybrid model. Real-time updates while browsing. Popularity fallback for cold start. A/B test algorithms. Track CTR+conversion. Daily model refresh.

When designing a REST API error response format, always include the following fields in the response body: a unique error code that can be used for programmatic error handling, a human-readable error message suitable for displaying to end users, a detailed technical description for developers that is only included in non-production environments, a request ID for correlation with server logs, and a link to documentation about the specific error. Use appropriate HTTP status codes and include the error details in the response body as JSON.
API error format (JSON): error_code (programmatic), message (user-facing), detail (dev-only, non-prod), request_id (log correlation), docs_url. Use correct HTTP status codes.

Implement a data migration from our legacy MySQL database to a new PostgreSQL database. The migration should:
- Map legacy schema to the new normalized schema design
- Transform and clean data during migration (fix encodings, normalize dates, deduplicate records)
- Maintain referential integrity by migrating tables in dependency order
- Support incremental migration with change data capture for the transition period
- Provide progress reporting and estimated completion time
- Include rollback capability if critical errors are detected
- Verify data integrity post-migration by comparing row counts and checksums
- Run in parallel where possible to minimize downtime

MySQL->PostgreSQL migration: map legacy to normalized schema, clean data (encodings/dates/dedup), migrate in dependency order, incremental CDC during transition, progress reporting + ETA, rollback on critical errors, verify integrity (counts+checksums), parallelize for speed.

You are an accessibility expert. When auditing web applications:
- Check all WCAG 2.1 AA success criteria
- Verify keyboard navigation works for all interactive elements
- Ensure screen reader compatibility with proper ARIA labels and roles
- Check color contrast ratios meet minimum requirements (4.5:1 for normal text, 3:1 for large text)
- Verify all images have meaningful alt text
- Test with real assistive technologies, not just automated tools
- Ensure focus indicators are visible
- Check that all functionality is available without relying solely on color

A11y audit: WCAG 2.1 AA, keyboard nav for all interactive elements, screen reader compat (ARIA), contrast (4.5:1 normal, 3:1 large), meaningful alt text, test with real AT (not just automated), visible focus indicators, no color-only functionality.

Implement a distributed locking mechanism for our microservices that need to coordinate access to shared resources. Use Redis as the lock store with the Redlock algorithm for multi-node Redis setups. Locks should have configurable TTL to prevent deadlocks from crashed processes. Implement lock extension for long-running operations. Add monitoring for lock contention and hold times. Include a circuit breaker that falls back to local locking if Redis is unavailable.

Distributed locking: Redis + Redlock for multi-node. Configurable TTL (prevent deadlocks). Lock extension for long ops. Monitor contention + hold times. Circuit breaker -> local lock fallback if Redis down.

Implement a command-line interface for our developer tools using the following requirements:
- Use a subcommand pattern (tool init, tool deploy, tool status)
- Support both flags and positional arguments
- Implement shell completion for bash, zsh, and fish
- Add colored output with a --no-color flag for CI environments
- Include a verbose mode with -v flag (stackable: -vv, -vvv)
- Show progress bars for long-running operations
- Support configuration via config file, environment variables, and flags (in that priority order)
- Add a --dry-run option for destructive commands

CLI: subcommand pattern, flags + positional args, shell completions (bash/zsh/fish), colored output (--no-color for CI), stackable -v verbosity, progress bars, config priority: file > env > flags, --dry-run for destructive ops.

Design a notification preference system where users can control which notifications they receive through which channels. Support email, push, SMS, and in-app channels. Allow granular control per notification type (marketing, transactional, system alerts, social). Respect quiet hours settings. Implement an escalation path where critical alerts bypass quiet hours. Default new users to essential notifications only.
Notification prefs: per-type (marketing/transactional/system/social) x per-channel (email/push/SMS/in-app). Quiet hours with critical alert bypass. New users default to essentials only.

There is a critical bug in the parseUserInput() function located in src/lib/validators/input.ts at line 142. The function is supposed to sanitize HTML entities but it's only escaping angle brackets and not handling ampersands or quotes. This causes an XSS vulnerability when user input is rendered in the dashboard component at src/components/Dashboard.tsx. Please fix parseUserInput() and add test cases in src/__tests__/validators/input.test.ts.

Bug in parseUserInput() at src/lib/validators/input.ts:142. Only escapes angle brackets, missing ampersands + quotes -> XSS in src/components/Dashboard.tsx. Fix parseUserInput() and add tests in src/__tests__/validators/input.test.ts.

I would really appreciate it if you could help me design and implement a comprehensive observability stack for our production Kubernetes cluster that is running approximately 47 microservices across 3 availability zones. We need to set up distributed tracing using OpenTelemetry with Jaeger as the backend, structured logging with the ELK stack (Elasticsearch 8.12, Logstash, and Kibana), and metrics collection using Prometheus with Grafana dashboards. The tracing should capture the full request lifecycle across all service boundaries with proper context propagation. For logging, we need to ensure that all services emit structured JSON logs with correlation IDs that link to the distributed traces. Metrics should include RED metrics (Rate, Errors, Duration) for every service, system-level metrics from node exporters, and custom business metrics. We also need alerting rules in Prometheus Alertmanager that page on-call engineers via PagerDuty for P1 incidents and send Slack notifications for P2-P4 issues.

Observability for prod K8s (47 microservices, 3 AZs): tracing via OpenTelemetry + Jaeger (full request lifecycle, cross-service context propagation), logging via ELK (Elasticsearch 8.12/Logstash/Kibana) with structured JSON + correlation IDs linked to traces, metrics via Prometheus + Grafana (RED per service, node exporters, custom business metrics). Alertmanager: PagerDuty for P1, Slack for P2-P4.

We are planning to migrate our entire monolithic Ruby on Rails 7.1 application to a Go-based microservices architecture using gRPC for inter-service communication and Protocol Buffers for serialization. The current application handles approximately 12,000 requests per second at peak load and has a PostgreSQL 16 database with roughly 340 tables and 2.8 terabytes of data. We need a detailed migration strategy that includes service boundary identification using domain-driven design principles, a strangler fig pattern implementation plan, data migration approach that supports running both systems simultaneously during the transition period which we estimate will take 18 months, and comprehensive testing strategy including contract testing between the new services. The budget for this migration is $2.4 million and we have a team of 16 engineers available.

Migrate monolithic Rails 7.1 -> Go microservices (gRPC + protobuf). Current: 12K rps peak, PostgreSQL 16 (340 tables, 2.8TB). Need: DDD service boundaries, strangler fig plan, dual-run data migration over 18mo transition, contract testing. Budget $2.4M, 16 engineers.

I need you to help me architect and build a real-time collaborative document editing system similar to Google Docs. The system needs to support Conflict-free Replicated Data Types (CRDTs) for handling concurrent edits without a central authority, WebSocket connections for real-time synchronization between clients, operational transformation as a fallback mechanism, presence indicators showing which users are currently viewing or editing the document, cursor position sharing so users can see where other collaborators are typing, an undo/redo system that works correctly in a multi-user environment, offline support with automatic conflict resolution when reconnecting, and version history with the ability to restore any previous version. The system should scale to support at least 500 concurrent editors per document and handle documents up to 10MB in size.

Build real-time collaborative editor (Google Docs-like): CRDTs for concurrent edits, WebSocket sync, OT fallback, presence indicators, cursor sharing, multi-user undo/redo, offline support with auto conflict resolution on reconnect, version history + restore. Scale: 500 concurrent editors/doc, docs up to 10MB.

I'm getting this error when running our integration tests:

Error: ECONNREFUSED 127.0.0.1:5432
    at TCPConnectWrap.afterConnect [as oncomplete] (net.js:1141:16)
    at Pool._handleError (node_modules/pg-pool/index.js:132:28)
    at Connection.emit (events.js:315:20)

The PostgreSQL container should be running but the tests keep failing in CI. Locally everything works fine. The docker-compose.ci.yml file starts postgres on port 5432 but maybe there's a timing issue.

Integration test error: ECONNREFUSED 127.0.0.1:5432 (pg-pool). PostgreSQL container should be running, works locally but fails in CI. docker-compose.ci.yml starts postgres:5432. Likely timing issue - container not ready when tests start.

Our production API is returning 502 Bad Gateway errors intermittently. The Nginx access logs show these requests timing out after 60 seconds. The application logs show:

[2026-03-26T14:22:33Z] WARN pool-1-thread-47: Database connection pool exhausted. Active: 50/50, Waiting: 127
[2026-03-26T14:22:34Z] ERROR pool-1-thread-12: java.sql.SQLTransientConnectionException: HikariPool-1 - Connection is not available, request timed out after 30000ms

The HikariCP pool is maxed out at 50 connections but we're seeing 127 queued requests. This started after yesterday's deploy that added a new reporting endpoint.

Prod 502s intermittent, Nginx timing out at 60s. Logs: HikariCP pool exhausted (50/50 active, 127 waiting), SQLTransientConnectionException after 30s timeout. Started after yesterday's deploy adding reporting endpoint. Likely new endpoint holds connections too long.

Help me build a feature engineering pipeline for our machine learning model that predicts customer churn. The raw data is in a Snowflake data warehouse and includes customer demographics, transaction history over the past 24 months, support ticket records, and product usage telemetry. We need to compute rolling window aggregates (7-day, 30-day, 90-day) for transaction amounts and frequencies, extract TF-IDF features from support ticket text, calculate recency-frequency-monetary (RFM) scores, and generate interaction features between demographic segments and usage patterns. Use Apache Spark on Databricks for the heavy transformations and store the feature vectors in a Feast feature store for both training and online serving.

Churn prediction feature pipeline: source from Snowflake (demographics, 24mo transactions, support tickets, usage telemetry). Compute rolling aggregates (7/30/90d) for txn amounts+frequency, TF-IDF from ticket text, RFM scores, interaction features (demographics x usage). Spark on Databricks for transforms, store in Feast for training + online serving.

If the user provides a CSV file, parse it and return the results as a formatted table. When the user asks for a code review, first check if they've provided the code inline or referenced a file. If they referenced a file, read it first. If the code is longer than 200 lines, focus on the most critical issues rather than doing a line-by-line review. Unless the user asks for a specific focus area, review for security, performance, and correctness in that priority order. If you find no issues, say so explicitly rather than inventing minor nitpicks.

Code review: if file referenced, read first. >200 lines: focus on critical issues, not line-by-line. Unless user specifies focus, prioritize: security > performance > correctness. If no issues found, say so explicitly (no invented nitpicks).

If the user switches languages mid-conversation, follow their lead and switch as well. Unless explicitly asked to translate, never auto-translate code comments or string literals.
Match user's language. If they switch, follow. Preserve original var names + comments in code. Never auto-translate code comments/string literals unless asked.
